title: Massage for rehabilitation after total knee arthroplasty: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.

items.ai_reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as 'a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials'.
Background describes KOA, TKA, complications, and notes no prior systematic review on massage for TKA rehabilitation, establishing rationale.
Explicitly states the objective: 'to assess the effect of massage on the rehabilitation of patients undergoing TKA surgery'.
Selection criteria section details PICOS-based inclusion criteria and exclusion criteria.
Lists PubMed, Web of Science, EMBASE, Cochrane Library, and CNKI, searched from inception to May 2024.
Search terms are listed and full search strategy is provided in Supplementary Table S1.
Two reviewers (Jin YY, Chen RN) independently screened literature with disagreements resolved by discussion or third-party researcher.
Two researchers independently extracted data with disagreements resolved by discussion or a third-party researcher.
Outcomes listed: postoperative pain, knee ROM, D-dimer levels, length of hospital stay, and adverse events, with specified time points.
Specifies extracted variables: first author, year, country, age, sex, sample size, intervention type/duration, and time points.
Cochrane Risk of Bias Assessment Tool was used, covering seven domains. However, reviewer independence for bias assessment is not explicitly stated but tool and process specified.
Specifies mean difference (MD) or standardized mean difference (SMD) with 95% CI as effect measures.
The paper does not explicitly describe a process for deciding which studies were eligible for each specific synthesis.
No description of methods for preparing data, handling missing summary statistics, or data conversions is provided.
Describes use of forest plots and tables to display individual study and synthesis results.
Describes fixed-effects model when I²<50% and random-effects when I²>50%, using Q statistic and I², with Review Manager 5.3 and Stata 16.0.
Subgroup analyses based on country type were performed to explore sources of heterogeneity.
The paper states sensitivity analysis was not performed due to small number of studies; no sensitivity analysis methods were described.
Publication bias was assessed using Begg's and Egger's tests.
No formal certainty assessment method (e.g., GRADE) is described; authors only subjectively mention 'low- to moderate-quality evidence' without a method.
Study selection results are described with numbers at each stage, and a PRISMA flow diagram (Fig. 1) is referenced.
Reasons for excluding 10 full-text articles are provided (not RCT, full text unavailable, wrong intervention, data not integrable).
Table 1 summarizes characteristics of each included study, citing all 11 studies.
Risk of bias assessments are presented in the text and in Fig. 2.
Forest plots (Figs. 3-6) present effect estimates with confidence intervals for each study and outcome.
Synthesis results do not explicitly summarize risk of bias among contributing studies for each synthesis.
Summary estimates with 95% CIs, p-values, and I² heterogeneity measures are presented for all syntheses.
Subgroup analysis results by country for length of hospital stay are presented (Fig. 7).
No sensitivity analyses were conducted, and no results are presented.
Results of Begg's and Egger's tests for publication bias are reported, with Egger's p=0.049 for day 14 pain.
No formal certainty assessment (e.g., GRADE) results are presented for each outcome.
Discussion interprets results regarding pain, ROM, and D-dimer in context of mechanisms and other evidence.
Limitations of evidence discussed: most studies from China, difficulty blinding, low-moderate quality of included studies.
Review process limitations discussed: potential publication bias, inability to perform sensitivity analyses due to few studies.
Discusses clinical implications (adjunctive treatment) and future research needs (larger, higher-quality trials, standardized massage methods).
Registered with PROSPERO, registration number CRD42023411680.
Although PROSPERO registration is noted, no explicit statement about where the protocol can be accessed is given.
No amendments to registration or protocol are described or addressed.
Funding section states 'Not applicable.'
Authors declare no competing interests.
States datasets are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.
Title explicitly identifies the report as 'a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials'.
The abstract states the objective: 'to evaluate the effectiveness of massage for postoperative rehabilitation after total knee arthroplasty (TKA)'.
Only mentions inclusion of RCTs on massage for postoperative TKA rehabilitation, but does not specify detailed inclusion/exclusion criteria (e.g., population, comparator specifics, exclusions).
The abstract specifies databases (PubMed, Web of Science, EMBASE, Cochrane Library, CNKI) and the search date (from inception to May 2024).
States that 'The Cochrane Risk of Bias Assessment Tool was used to assess the risk of bias'.
Mentions that a meta-analysis was performed and subgroup analysis conducted, but does not specify the statistical methods used to synthesize results (e.g., fixed/random effects, effect measures).
Reports 'Eleven randomized controlled clinical trials with 940 subjects were included'.
Reports direction of effects for outcomes but does not provide summary estimates, confidence intervals, or number of studies/participants per outcome from the meta-analysis.
Mentions 'the heterogeneity of the studies was large' but does not provide a clear summary of the limitations of evidence (e.g., risk of bias, imprecision).
Provides general interpretation: 'massage can be used as an adjunctive treatment for rehabilitation after TKA'.
The abstract does not mention any source of funding for the review.
The abstract does not provide any register name or registration number (e.g., PROSPERO).

title: Parameters of Spinal Cord Stimulation in Complex Regional Pain Syndrome: Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.

items.ai_reason: Title explicitly includes 'Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials'
Introduction describes CRPS, SCS mechanisms, and notes that a review of SCS parameters for CRPS in RCTs has not been conducted, providing context
Study explicitly states three aims: characterizing SCS waveforms/parameters, determining preferred waveform, and providing a narrative review of SCS use in RCTs for CRPS
Inclusion criteria (RCTs with CRPS patients treated with various SCS waveforms) and exclusion criteria (case studies, protocol descriptions, non-randomized studies) are explicitly stated
PubMed, Embase, and CINHLA are listed as databases, but the date of last search is not specified
Only general keywords 'Complex Regional Pain Syndrome' AND 'Spinal Cord Stimulation' are mentioned; full search strategies with filters and limits for each database are not presented
The paper does not specify how many reviewers screened records, whether they worked independently, or any automation tool details for study selection
Methods for data collection, number of reviewers, and whether they worked independently are not described
Primary outcome of pain relief via VAS and GPE is specified, with note that other similar pain measurement scales were considered
Paper states recording number of patients, treatment modalities, and SCS parameters (frequency, pulse width, amplitude) for control and intervention groups
Cochrane RoB 2 tool was used with two independent reviewers and disagreements resolved by discussion or third author
Weighted mean difference (MD) in pain scores and GPE with 95% CI specified as the effect measure
The process for deciding which studies were eligible for each synthesis is not explicitly described beyond mentioning that only LF-SCS vs conventional/sham could be pooled
No description of methods for handling missing summary statistics or data conversions
Forest plots and tables are used to display individual study and synthesis results, as referenced in figures and tables
DerSimonian and Laird random effects meta-analysis described with rationale (heterogeneity), and RevMan 5.4 software specified
No methods described to explore causes of heterogeneity (e.g., subgroup analysis, meta-regression)
Sensitivity analysis by excluding studies in stepwise manner is described
No methods described for assessing risk of bias due to missing results or reporting biases in synthesis
No methods (e.g., GRADE) described for assessing certainty in the body of evidence
Results describe 99 articles initially gathered, 8 duplicates removed, 91 screened, 4 included; Figure 1 referenced as flow diagram
Studies that might appear to meet inclusion criteria but were excluded are not cited with explanations
Each included study is cited and characteristics are presented in Table 1 and in narrative form
Risk of bias assessments for each included study are reported in the Risk of Bias Assessment section and referenced Figure 2
VAS scores with means and standard deviations are presented for individual studies, and forest plots (Fig 3) display effect estimates
The narrative describes characteristics and notes risk of bias levels for contributing studies in each synthesis
Meta-analysis results are presented with MD, 95% CI, P-value, and I² for both pain reduction and GPE outcomes
No investigations of causes of heterogeneity (e.g., subgroup analyses) are presented
Sensitivity analysis results presented in Table 2 and Figure 4 showing effect of excluding individual studies
No assessments of risk of bias due to missing results from reporting biases are presented
No certainty of evidence assessment (e.g., GRADE) is presented for outcomes
Discussion interprets findings in context of prior reviews (Bruehl 2015) and other evidence on SCS efficacy
Limitations of evidence discussed, including mixed neuropathic pain populations, incomplete SCS parameter reporting, and limited long-term data
Limitations section discusses review process limitations including not prompting databases beyond RCTs and limited follow-up timepoints
Discussion includes implications for practice and recommendations for future research (e.g., stratifying by pain pathology, longer follow-up)
No registration information provided and no statement that the review was not registered
No information about where the protocol can be accessed, nor statement that protocol was not prepared
No amendments to registration/protocol are described
Disclaimer states 'There was no external funding in the preparation of this manuscript'
Authors declare no commercial associations that might pose a conflict of interest
No statement about availability of data collection forms, extracted data, analytic code, or other review materials
Title explicitly identifies the report as 'Systematic Review and Meta-analysis'.
Objective is stated: 'To provide evidence for the use of SCS to treat CRPS and characterize the additional benefits of various SCS waveforms.'
Inclusion criteria specified: randomized controlled trials comparing SCS parameters for the treatment of CRPS.
Information sources specified: PubMed, Embase, and CINHLA. However, dates of last search not provided, but databases are stated.
Risk of bias assessment is reported with ratings (low, some concerns, high risk of bias) though specific tool name not mentioned.
Describes use of meta-analysis with mean differences, confidence intervals, and I² heterogeneity statistics for synthesis.
Reports four RCTs included and describes their comparison arms, though total participant numbers are not provided.
Main outcomes reported with summary estimates (MD = -1.17, 95% CI = -1.61 to -0.73) and direction favoring LF-SCS.
Limitations noted: pooled analysis with different RCT designs, inclusion of multiple neuropathic pathologies, and one study at high risk of bias.
Interpretation provided: LF-SCS is superior to conventional therapy/placebo, with implications that more evidence is needed for novel SCS parameters.
No mention of funding source in the abstract.
No registration name or registration number is provided in the abstract.

title: Is the combination of exercise therapy and health education more effective than usual medical care in the prevention of non-specific back pain? A systematic review with meta-analysis.

items.ai_reason: Title labels the paper as 'SYSTEMATIC REVIEW' and abstract describes it as a systematic review and meta-analysis.
Introduction describes prevalence and burden of NBP, existing evidence on ET and HE separately, and gap regarding combined interventions versus usual care.
Explicitly states primary objective to determine whether ET+HE is more effective than UC for NBP prevention, with secondary objectives on disability and kinesiophobia.
Inclusion criteria (publication years, intervention, sample, control) and exclusion criteria (design, specific causes, pregnancy, no full text) are specified.
PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science and Medline specified with search conducted in December 2021.
Table 1 presents full search equations for each database including MeSH terms and Boolean operators.
Two reviewers (P.H.-L and J.L.-B.) independently screened articles with disagreements resolved by debate until agreement.
Two reviewers independently extracted data using a customized Excel form, with disagreements resolved by discussion.
Outcomes specified as pain characteristics, disability, and kinesiophobia; data items listed including variables analyzed and instruments used.
Lists data extracted: demographics, sample characteristics (age, gender, criteria, n), study parameters (duration, adverse events, intervention methods), and follow-up time.
Jadad and PEDro scales used; two reviewers independently extracted data. Though reviewer number for bias assessment isn't explicitly stated, the tools are specified.
Standardized mean differences (SMD) with 95% CI specified as the effect measure, with cut-off interpretations provided.
The paper does not explicitly describe the process for deciding which studies were eligible for each specific synthesis.
No description of methods to handle missing summary statistics or data conversions for preparation of synthesis.
States tables were used to describe study characteristics and extracted data; forest plots used to display meta-analysis results.
Describes SMD calculation, I² for heterogeneity with thresholds, significance level, and software (Comprehensive Meta-Analysis V2). Model not explicitly named but synthesis method described.
No description of subgroup analysis or meta-regression methods to explore heterogeneity.
No sensitivity analyses are described in the methods.
No methods described to assess risk of bias due to missing results or reporting biases (e.g., funnel plot, Egger's test).
No methods described for assessing certainty of evidence (e.g., GRADE).
Reports 8414 records, 1832 after duplicates, 1531 excluded, 4 included; PRISMA flow diagram presented in Figure 1.
Does not cite specific studies that appeared to meet inclusion criteria but were excluded with reasons.
Tables 3 and 4 cite each of the 4 included studies with characteristics (sample, criteria, intervention, etc.).
Table 2 presents PEDro scale assessments and Table 3 presents Jadad scale assessments for each included study.
Table 4 presents results for each study; forest plots (Figure 2) show effect estimates with confidence intervals.
Summarises characteristics (sample sizes, interventions) and methodological quality (PEDro 6.5 average) of contributing studies.
Presents SMD, 95% CI, p-values, and I² heterogeneity measures for pain and disability syntheses, with direction of effect described.
Heterogeneity is reported quantitatively but no investigation of possible causes (e.g., subgroup analysis) is presented.
No sensitivity analyses were conducted or reported.
No assessment of risk of bias due to missing results/reporting biases is presented.
No certainty of evidence assessment (e.g., GRADE) is presented.
Discussion interprets findings in context of prior reviews on ET and HE, citing multiple related studies.
Limitations section notes high heterogeneity prevented identification of most effective intervention type, frequency, and duration.
Acknowledges limitations of review: no differentiated analysis by gender/age subgroups, and no inclusion of studies in pregnant women.
States results may help healthcare professionals improve clinical interventions and calls for further research on specific protocols for NBP prevention.
Registered on PROSPERO with ID CRD42022311026 (also CRD42021290875 noted at beginning).
Mentions PROSPERO registration but does not explicitly indicate where the protocol itself can be accessed.
No description of amendments to the protocol or registration information.
Funding statement explicitly states 'there is no funding associated with the work featured in this article.'
Disclosure statement reports 'No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).'
Data availability statement indicates dataset is available from the corresponding author.
Title explicitly identifies the report as 'A systematic review with meta-analysis.'
The abstract explicitly states the aim: to determine if the combination of exercise plus education is more effective for the prevention of non-specific back pain than usual medical care.
Inclusion criteria are specified (articles published 2016-2021, intervention included exercise and education, non-specific back pain patients), though exclusion criteria are not explicitly stated.
Information sources are specified: PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science and Medline. However, the date of last search is not provided.
The abstract mentions PEDro score (6.5 points average) but does not explicitly describe this as the risk of bias assessment method in the abstract.
The abstract mentions meta-analysis and standardized mean differences but does not specify the synthesis methods (e.g., statistical model, software, heterogeneity assessment).
The abstract reports 4 randomized controlled trials included but does not provide the total number of participants or summarize relevant characteristics of the studies.
Results for main outcomes reported with SMDs and 95% CIs for pain intensity (-0.75, -1.41 to -0.08) and disability (-0.24, -0.38 to -0.1), with direction favoring exercise plus education.
No discussion of limitations of the evidence (e.g., risk of bias, inconsistency, imprecision) is provided in the abstract.
A general interpretation is provided: 'Interventions combining exercise and education seem to have a greater preventive effect on non-specific back pain than usual medical care.'
No information about the primary source of funding for the review is mentioned in the abstract.
No register name or registration number is provided in the abstract.

title: Effect of Repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation on Post-stroke Dysphagia: A Meta-analysis of Stimulation Frequency, Stimulation Site, and Timing of Outcome Measurement.

items.ai_reason: Title does not include 'systematic review'; it only indicates it is an original article on rTMS for post-stroke dysphagia and mentions meta-analysis in keywords.
Introduction explains existing evidence and limitations of prior meta-analyses (not adjusting for stimulation frequency, site, timing), establishing rationale for the current review.
Explicit objective stated: evaluate rTMS effects on post-stroke dysphagia by stimulation frequency, stimulation site, and timing of outcome measurement.
Eligibility section lists explicit inclusion (5 criteria) and exclusion criteria (reviews, case reports, letters, crossover, insufficient data) and groupings by frequency/site/timing.
Databases specified (PubMed, SCOPUS, Embase, Web of Science, Cochrane Library) with search dates from January 1, 1980 to December 13, 2021.
Keywords listed and authors state the full search strategy is described in Sect. 1 (supplementary), with filters for human RCTs and English language noted.
Two independent reviewers (YJC and MCC) screened titles/abstracts and full texts, with disagreements resolved by consensus.
Two reviewers independently extracted data; corresponding authors were contacted for missing data, and graph data extracted with a ruler when needed.
Outcomes specified as SSA and PAS scores, measured immediately, at 3 weeks, and at 4 weeks after rTMS sessions; only these two measures were analyzed.
Variables extracted listed: first author, year, sample size, demographic data, rTMS protocol, and outcome measures (means and SDs); assumptions about correlation described.
Risk of bias assessed using Cochrane Collaboration Handbook tool across specified domains; discrepancies resolved by consensus, though number of independent reviewers not explicitly stated.
Standardized mean difference (SMD) with 95% CI specified as the effect measure for the synthesis.
Inclusion criterion #5 specifies that a measurement tool must be used in two or more studies for meta-analysis; groupings by frequency, site, and timing described.
PAS ordinal variable converted to parametric data described; formulas for SMD and pooled SD provided; missing correlations calculated from other articles.
Results tabulated in Table 1 for study characteristics and forest plots (Figs. 3, 4) displayed for syntheses.
Fixed-effects model used with rationale (assumption of same effect size); Cochran's Q test for heterogeneity; RevMan 5.3 software specified.
No subgroup analysis, meta-regression, or other methods to explore heterogeneity described beyond pre-specified groupings; no dedicated heterogeneity exploration methodology reported.
No sensitivity analyses are described in the methods section.
No methods described for assessing risk of bias due to missing results (e.g., funnel plots, Egger's test) from reporting biases.
No methods described for assessing certainty or confidence in the body of evidence (e.g., GRADE).
Flow described: 566 identified, 194 duplicates excluded, 335 excluded after title/abstract screening, remaining full-text reviewed, 6 included; Fig. 1 flowchart referenced.
Discussion explicitly cites excluded studies (Khedr et al., Park et al., Cheng et al.) with reasons for exclusion (missing SD, contralesional high-frequency rTMS, different outcome measures).
Table 1 cites each of the six included studies and presents study characteristics (design, participants, rTMS protocol, controls, follow-up, outcomes).
Risk of bias assessments for included studies presented in text and Fig. 2 across Cochrane domains.
Individual study summary statistics and effect estimates with precision are not presented for each study; only pooled SMDs shown. Forest plots referenced but individual data not explicitly tabulated.
Characteristics of contributing studies summarized for each synthesis (number of studies, frequency, site, timing) with risk of bias described narratively.
Pooled SMDs with 95% CIs and P-values presented for each meta-analysis; heterogeneity (Cochran's Q) reported as not significant; directions of effect described.
No investigations of causes of heterogeneity presented (Q test was not significant, and no subgroup/meta-regression analyses were conducted to explore heterogeneity).
No sensitivity analyses are presented.
No assessment of risk of bias due to missing results (reporting biases) is presented.
No certainty of evidence assessment (e.g., GRADE) presented for outcomes.
Discussion interprets findings in the context of prior meta-analyses (Liao, Cheng, Li et al.) and prior RCTs, comparing effect sizes.
Limitations of included evidence discussed: small study number, co-interventions in control groups (Lim), inclusion of a TBI patient (Kim), heterogeneous protocols.
Review process limitations discussed: exclusion of studies not fitting interhemispheric inhibition model and those using other outcome measures.
Implications discussed: rTMS as therapeutic option, need for high-quality RCTs with common outcomes, larger samples, and bilateral rTMS investigations.
No registration information is provided, and the paper does not state whether the review was registered.
No information on protocol availability is provided, nor a statement that no protocol was prepared.
No amendments to protocol/registration discussed as no protocol/registration mentioned.
Funding section states no external funding but supported by National Research Foundation of Korea grant (NRF-2019M3E5D1A02068106); role of funder not explicitly described but support disclosed.
Declaration states 'The authors declare no conflict of interest.'
No statement on availability of data, code, data collection forms, or analytic materials beyond the supplementary file reference.
Title explicitly includes 'A Meta-analysis', identifying the report as a systematic review/meta-analysis.
Abstract explicitly states the objective: to evaluate the effect of rTMS parameters (frequency, site, timing) on post-stroke dysphagia.
Abstract mentions inclusion of RCTs on rTMS for post-stroke dysphagia but does not specify detailed inclusion/exclusion criteria.
Abstract specifies databases (PubMed, SCOPUS, Embase, Cochrane Library) and search dates (January 01, 1980, to December 13, 2021).
Abstract does not mention any method used to assess risk of bias in included studies.
Abstract indicates results were synthesized using standardized mean difference (SMD) with 95% CI, implying meta-analytic synthesis methods.
Abstract reports six included studies with 158 patients (81 rTMS, 77 sham) and mentions RCT design.
Abstract presents SMDs with 95% CIs and P-values for SSA and PAS outcomes at multiple time points, indicating direction of effect.
Abstract does not discuss limitations of the evidence, such as risk of bias, inconsistency, or imprecision.
Abstract concludes that both high-frequency ipsilesional and low-frequency contralesional rTMS improved swallowing function, providing general interpretation.
Abstract does not mention any funding source for the review.
Abstract does not provide any register name or registration number.

title: Do Patients with Prostate Cancer Benefit from Exercise Interventions? A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.

items.ai_reason: Title explicitly states 'A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis'.
Introduction describes rationale, existing evidence gaps, and justifies the need for an updated review given doubled number of trials since 2015.
Explicit objective stated: to review effects of exercise interventions on QoL and metabolic health and examine differential effects across exercise modalities.
Section 2.2 details inclusion criteria (RCTs, population, intervention, comparator) and synthesis grouping by exercise modality (aerobic, resistance, combined).
Lists nine databases (MEDLINE, EMBASE, CENTRAL, CINAHL, PsycINFO, SPORTDiscus, PEDro, Scopus, OpenGrey) and ClinicalTrials.gov with search dates provided.
Supplementary Material File S1 contains the complete MEDLINE search strategy.
Section 2.4 describes two independent reviewers (M.F.A. and E.D.B.) using a three-step process with third author (J.M.) resolving disagreements.
Section 2.5 states M.F.A. extracted data and E.D.B. independently checked for accuracy.
Outcomes listed: cancer-specific QoL, cardiovascular fitness, fatigue, general physical/mental health, lower body strength, lean body mass, fat mass, blood pressure; time point selection specified.
Study characteristics table reports participant, intervention characteristics; assumptions about SD (using baseline SD or calculated from SE) are described.
Section 2.7 specifies Cochrane risk of bias tool with two excluded domains justified. However, number of independent reviewers not specified.
Section 2.8 specifies WMD for identical instruments and SMD for different instruments with 95% CI.
Stratification into aerobic, resistance, and combined exercise for each outcome described in methods.
Section 2.8 describes handling of missing post-intervention SD using baseline SD or calculated from SE; splitting control groups for multi-arm trials.
Tables of study characteristics and forest plots used to visually display results, described in methods and shown in results.
Section 2.9 describes random effects model with rationale, chi-squared and I-squared for heterogeneity, and STATA 15.1 software.
Section 2.11 describes subgroup analyses by intervention modality and by assessment instrument type to explore heterogeneity.
Section 2.11 describes sensitivity analyses: change score values, random vs fixed effect, excluding cluster-randomised, pilot/feasibility studies, and outliers.
Section 2.10 describes funnel plot and Egger's test for publication bias when >10 studies available.
No method for assessing certainty of evidence (e.g., GRADE) is described.
Flow diagram (Figure 1) presents search and selection process: 6693 identified records, 33 included trials.
The review does not cite specific studies that appeared eligible but were excluded, nor provide reasons for their exclusion.
Table 1 cites each included study and presents detailed characteristics (population, intervention, duration, frequency, control, outcomes).
Supplementary Material Files S2 and S3 present risk of bias summary table and risk of bias with reason for included studies.
Forest plots (Figures 2, 3 and supplementary figures) present individual study effect estimates with confidence intervals for each outcome.
Results section summarises characteristics of contributing studies for each synthesis (e.g., number of trials, participants, intervention arms).
Pooled SMDs/WMDs with 95% CIs and I-squared heterogeneity statistics are presented for each synthesis with direction of effect.
Subgroup analyses by exercise modality and assessment instrument are presented in supplementary figures and discussed in results.
Although sensitivity analyses were prespecified (change scores, fixed vs random, excluding clusters/pilots/outliers), results are not clearly reported beyond a change score figure in supplementary.
Funnel plots and Egger's test results reported for QoL and cardiovascular fitness syntheses in supplementary material.
No certainty of evidence assessment (e.g., GRADE) is reported for any outcome.
Discussion interprets findings in context of prior reviews (Bourke et al., Lopez et al.) and related studies.
Limitations section discusses within-study heterogeneity, underrepresentation of certain patient groups, and limited adherence/fidelity reporting.
Limitations mention inability to conduct subgroup analyses, exclusion of low-intensity exercise trials, and not investigating moderator effects of exercise components.
Discussion and conclusion offer implications for practice (exercise complementary to treatment) and future research directions (combined interventions, optimal dose, targeting suboptimal baselines).
Section 2.1 states the protocol was registered at PROSPERO with registration number CRD42020153851.
PROSPERO registration (CRD42020153851) provides access to the protocol.
No amendments to the protocol or registration information are described.
Funding statement: 'This research received no external funding.'
Conflicts of Interest statement: 'The authors declare no conflict of interest.'
Data Availability Statement indicates data can be requested from corresponding author; supplementary files contain search strategy, risk of bias, and forest plots.
Title explicitly states 'A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis'.
States 'We aimed to review the effect of exercise on QoL and metabolic health in a broad prostate cancer population.'
Abstract mentions 'eligible trials' and randomised controlled trials but does not specify inclusion/exclusion criteria such as population, intervention, or outcomes details.
States 'nine databases' were searched but does not name the databases or specify the date each was last searched.
No mention of any method used to assess risk of bias in included studies.
States outcomes were stratified into aerobic, resistance, and combined exercise, and meta-analysis used SMD with 95% CI.
Reports 33 randomised controlled trials with 2567 participants included.
Reports SMDs and 95% CIs for main outcomes (QoL, cardiovascular fitness, aerobic exercise modality) with direction of effect.
No discussion of limitations of the evidence such as risk of bias, inconsistency, or imprecision.
Concludes exercise is effective for metabolic health with aerobic being superior, and small effect on QoL not mediated by modality.
No information about funding source is provided in the abstract.
No registration name or number is provided in the abstract.

title: Effects of High-Intensity Strength Training in Adults With Knee Osteoarthritis: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.

items.ai_reason: Title identifies the report as 'A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials'.
Introduction describes controversy regarding effects of high-intensity strength training vs low-intensity training/routine care, citing conflicting prior studies as the rationale.
Objective explicitly stated: to assess effects of high-intensity strength training vs low-intensity training or routine care on knee pain, function, quality of life, and adverse events.
Inclusion and exclusion criteria are explicitly listed including population, intervention, comparator, outcomes, study design, and language.
Four databases (PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, Web of Science) specified with search date up to March 10, 2021.
Full search terms listed in text and Supplementary Table 1 shows PubMed search strategy.
States two authors independently screened studies with disagreements resolved by discussion; Endnote X9 used for screening.
States data extraction was conducted by two authors independently with disagreement resolved through discussion.
Outcomes (knee pain, function, quality of life, adverse events) and their specific measurement instruments are listed.
Variables collected listed: author, year, country, design, intervention, sample size, age, sex, BMI, follow-up, Jadad score, outcomes.
Risk of bias assessed using Cochrane RevMan 5.3 tool by two independent authors across seven specified domains.
Weighted mean differences specified for measurement data and relative risks for enumeration data with 95% CIs.
The paper does not explicitly describe the processes used to decide which studies were eligible for each specific synthesis.
No description of handling missing summary statistics or data conversions for synthesis preparation.
States forest plots were drawn for each outcome to visually display results.
Describes fixed/random effects model selection based on I² ≥50%, Q test, and STATA 15.1 software used.
No methods described for exploring causes of heterogeneity (e.g., subgroup analysis or meta-regression); discussion notes this was not analyzed.
States 'All outcomes were subjected to sensitivity analysis' by eliminating one study at a time.
No methods described for assessing risk of bias due to missing results or reporting biases (e.g., funnel plots, Egger's test).
No methods described for assessing certainty of evidence (e.g., GRADE).
Search and selection results described (1732 records, 809 after duplicates, 33 full-text, 10 included) with PRISMA flow diagram in Figure 1.
Does not cite specific studies that appeared to meet inclusion criteria but were excluded with reasons.
Included studies cited and characteristics presented in Supplementary Table 2.
Risk of bias for each included study presented in Figures 2A and 2B (summary and graph).
Forest plots present individual study results with effect estimates and confidence intervals.
Each synthesis section briefly notes the studies and participant numbers contributing; overall risk of bias summarized.
Results of all statistical syntheses presented with pooled WMDs/RRs, 95% CIs, I² values, and P values.
No investigations of heterogeneity causes (subgroup analysis, meta-regression) were conducted or presented; authors note this as limitation.
Sensitivity analysis results reported: 'the overall evaluation did not depend on a single study'.
No assessments of risk of bias due to missing results (reporting biases) presented.
No certainty of evidence assessments (e.g., GRADE) presented for outcomes.
Discussion interprets findings in context of prior reviews (Regnaux et al., Juhl et al., Thorstensson et al.).
Limitations of evidence discussed: self-reported outcomes, subjectivity, female-only studies, lack of definition of high/low intensity.
Review process limitations discussed: only English trials included, heterogeneity sources not analyzed, studies possibly excluded due to insufficient intervention description.
Discusses implications including need for future research on optimal intensity and definitions, and clinical application of high-intensity training.
No registration information provided and no statement that the review was not registered.
No information about protocol accessibility or statement that no protocol was prepared.
No discussion of amendments to protocol or registration.
No sources of financial or non-financial support for the review described.
States 'no conflicts of interest have been reported by the authors or by any individuals in control of the content of this article'.
No statement about public availability of data collection forms, extracted data, analytic code, or other review materials.
Title explicitly identifies the report as 'A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.'
The abstract states the aim is to assess effects of high-intensity vs low-intensity strength training or routine care in adults with knee osteoarthritis.
The abstract does not specify inclusion/exclusion criteria beyond implying RCTs in adults with knee osteoarthritis; no formal eligibility criteria are stated.
Information sources (PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, Web of Science) and last search date (March 10, 2021) are reported.
The abstract does not mention any method used to assess risk of bias in the included studies.
The abstract does not describe methods used to present or synthesize results (e.g., meta-analysis model, heterogeneity assessment).
Ten studies with 892 subjects with knee osteoarthritis were reported as included.
Results for main outcomes are reported, including a meta-analysis estimate for peak torque of knee flexors (WMD 7.520; 95% CI 1.256 to 13.784; P=0.019) favoring high-intensity.
No limitations of the evidence (risk of bias, inconsistency, imprecision) are discussed in the abstract.
A general interpretation is provided: high-intensity strength training may have similar effects and comparable safety to low-intensity training/routine care.
The abstract does not specify any source of funding for the review.
No registration name or registration number is provided in the abstract.

title: Meta-Analysis of the Effect of Yoga Practice on Physical Fitness in the Elderly.

items.ai_reason: Title explicitly states 'Meta-Analysis of the Effect of Yoga Practice on Physical Fitness in the Elderly,' identifying it as a meta-analysis/systematic review.
The introduction describes existing yoga meta-analyses and identifies the gap regarding yoga effects on physical fitness in the elderly, providing rationale.
Explicitly states: 'The purpose of this study was to meta-analyze the effects of yoga intervention on physical fitness in the elderly.'

Five specific inclusion criteria are listed (age >60, single yoga intervention, physical fitness outcome, pre-post experimental design, statistical values available). Grouping by physical fitness factors is described.
States Cochrane, PubMed, and Embase databases were searched on 25 March 2021.
Only mentions main search terms (yoga, the elderly, RCT research) without presenting full search strategies, filters, or Boolean combinations for each database.
States 'Two investigators independently selected studies for inclusion; disagreements were resolved by discussion.'
Describes coding items but does not specify how many reviewers collected data, whether independently, or processes for confirming data.
Physical fitness outcomes are listed and categorized into seven domains (upper/lower limb strength, upper/lower body flexibility, mobility, cardiorespiratory fitness, balance).
Lists coding items (author, year, participants, age, period) but does not describe assumptions made about missing or unclear information.
No risk of bias assessment methodology or tool (e.g., Cochrane RoB) is described for included studies.
Specifies Cohen's d as the effect size measure, with interpretation thresholds (small <0.40, moderate 0.40-0.70, large >0.70).
The coding section describes classification of outcome parameters into physical fitness factors for synthesis.
No description of methods to handle missing summary statistics or data conversions.
Results are displayed in tables (characteristics table, effect size tables) and funnel plots.
Describes use of fixed-effect or random-effect models based on heterogeneity, Q and I² for heterogeneity assessment, and CMA 3.0 software.
Subgroup analyses by physical fitness factor, age group (60s/70s/80s), and intervention period (<8, 9-12, >13 weeks) are described.
No sensitivity analyses are described (Trim and Fill is for publication bias, not sensitivity analysis per se).
Describes funnel plot and Egger's regression intercept to assess publication bias, with Trim and Fill for correction.
No method such as GRADE or other approach to assess certainty of evidence is described.
Reports 656 papers identified and 12 studies finally included, referencing a PRISMA flow diagram in Figure 1.
Does not cite specific excluded studies that might appear to meet criteria with reasons for exclusion.
Table 1 lists all 12 included studies with citations and characteristics (region, design, participants, intervention, outcomes).
No risk of bias assessment for individual studies is presented.
Individual study effect estimates with precision are not presented; only pooled and subgroup results are shown.
Brief summaries of contributing studies' characteristics by synthesis are presented in tables, but risk of bias among contributing studies is not summarized as no RoB was assessed.
Table 2 and Table 3 present summary estimates, 95% CIs, Q, and I² for overall and subgroup analyses.
Subgroup analyses by physical fitness factor, age, and period are presented in Table 3 and discussed as heterogeneity sources.
No sensitivity analyses are reported.
Publication bias is presented via funnel plot, Egger's test (T=3.50, p<0.05), and Trim and Fill correction (0.45 to 0.41).
No certainty/confidence assessment (e.g., GRADE) of the body of evidence is presented.
Discussion interprets results in context of other yoga research and provides general interpretation.
Limitations including unclear yoga type information in included papers and publication bias are discussed.
Section 5 'Limitations in Research' discusses review process limitations including categorical moderator variables and exclusion of non-RCT studies.
States physical activity policies should promote yoga and suggests directions for future research (prospective studies, yoga type analyses).
Registered on PROSPERO with registration number CRD42021247535.
Mentions a protocol was developed and registered on PROSPERO but does not provide a specific accessible link to the protocol document.
No amendments to registration or protocol are described.
Funding statement: 'This work was supported by the 2021 Research Fund of University of Ulsan.'
States: 'The authors declare no conflict of interest.'
Data Availability Statement is 'Not applicable'; no template data collection forms, extracted data, or analytic code are reported as available.
Title explicitly identifies the report as a 'Meta-Analysis', which is a form of systematic review.
The abstract states the purpose: 'to meta-analyze the effects of yoga intervention on physical fitness in the elderly'.
The abstract does not specify inclusion/exclusion criteria such as study types, population details, or intervention specifications.
The abstract specifies databases (Cochrane, PubMed, Embase) and the search date (25 March 2021).
No mention of methods used to assess risk of bias in the included studies.
The abstract does not describe synthesis methods such as effect size calculation, statistical model, or subgroup analysis methodology.
Total number of included studies (12) is given, but no participant numbers or study characteristics are summarised.
Results are described qualitatively (e.g., 'moderately positive effects') without numerical summary estimates, confidence intervals, or study/participant counts per outcome.
No discussion of limitations of the evidence (risk of bias, inconsistency, or imprecision) is provided.
The abstract provides general interpretation and implications, recommending yoga as a physical activity policy for elderly wellbeing.
No information about the primary source of funding is provided in the abstract.
No register name or registration number is mentioned in the abstract.

title: Systematic review and meta-analysis on the effectiveness of ultrasound-guided versus landmark corticosteroid injection in the treatment of shoulder pain: an update.

items.ai_reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as a 'Systematic review and meta-analysis'.
Introduction describes existing debate on US-guided vs landmark CS injections and the rationale for updating the evidence.
Explicitly states the objective to investigate the effectiveness and safety of US-guided versus landmark CS injections in adult shoulder pain patients.
Inclusion restricted to RCTs in English with adult patients; exclusions listed (observational studies, case reports, reviews, etc.).
Lists MEDLINE, Scopus, Web of Science, EBSCO, Cochrane Library, searched up to July 3, 2021, plus reference lists.
Keywords are listed and full search strategies are reported in the Supplementary file.
States two investigators independently and in duplicate screened title/abstract and full text of retrieved publications.
Describes use of a data collection form but does not specify how many reviewers extracted data, nor whether independently, nor processes for confirming data.
Lists all outcomes sought including VAS, DASH, SDQ, SPADI, CMS, Oxford, abduction degree, side effects, and cost-effectiveness with definitions.
Lists extracted variables: author, year, country, study design, sample size, age, female percentage, shoulder disease, function score, and follow-up.
Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool used with specified domains; disagreements resolved by consensus, implying multiple reviewers.
Specifies SMD, MD, and RR with 95% CIs as effect measures used in synthesis.
Does not explicitly describe the process for deciding which studies were eligible for each specific synthesis.
No description of methods to prepare data for synthesis such as handling of missing summary statistics or data conversions.
Describes use of forest plots and tables to display results of individual studies and syntheses.
Describes Mantel-Haenszel fixed-effects model, I² and Q for heterogeneity, and R software (meta package 4.9-0).
Describes subgroup analyses (e.g., by VAS scale type and functional score type) to explore heterogeneity.
States sensitivity analyses using Mantel-Haenszel fixed-effects models and leave-one-out meta-analysis were conducted.
Describes use of funnel plot method to assess publication bias among included studies.
No method (e.g., GRADE) was described to assess certainty or confidence in the body of evidence.
Reports 2281 records identified, 457 duplicates, 1824 screened, 30 full-text reviewed, 18 included, with PRISMA flow diagram (Fig. 1).
States a list of excluded studies with main reason for exclusion is reported in the Supplementary file.
Each included study is cited and characteristics presented in Table 1 (author, year, country, design, population, age, etc.).
Risk of bias assessment summarized in text and detailed in Supplementary file.
Forest plots (Figs. 2-6) present individual study results with effect estimates and confidence intervals.
Characteristics of contributing studies summarized per synthesis (number of studies, patients) and overall risk of bias described.
Presents pooled SMD/MD/RR with 95% CIs and I² heterogeneity measures for each synthesis, with direction of effect described.
Subgroup analyses presented for VAS (0-10 vs 0-100), functional score type, and disability score type to investigate heterogeneity.
Results of leave-one-out sensitivity analysis reported (no study affected overall estimate), detailed in Supplementary file.
Reports no significant publication bias observed, funnel plots symmetrical in all outcomes, with plots in Supplementary file.
No certainty assessment (e.g., GRADE) is presented for any outcome.
Discussion interprets results in context of prior meta-analyses by Sage et al., Soh et al., and other evidence.
Discusses limitations of included evidence: short follow-up, lack of blinding, small sample sizes.
Does not explicitly discuss limitations of the review processes (e.g., English-only restriction, search limitations).
Discusses implications for clinical practice and recommends further RCTs and cost-effectiveness research.
No registration information is provided, nor a statement that the review was not registered.
No information is provided about protocol access or its preparation.
No amendments to registration or protocol are described.
Funding statement mentions open access funding by STDF/EKB and 'None to declare' for other support.
Conflict of interest declaration: 'None to declare'.
Does not describe availability of data collection forms, extracted data, analytic code, or other materials beyond supplementary file contents.
Title explicitly identifies the report as 'Systematic review and meta-analysis'.
States objective: to assess effectiveness of US-guided versus landmark CS injections in adult patients with shoulder pain.
Inclusion criteria specified: RCTs comparing US-guided vs landmark CS injection in adult patients with shoulder pain, reporting specified outcomes.
Databases listed (MEDLINE, Scopus, Web of Science, EBSCO, Cochrane Library) but the date when each was last searched is not provided.
Abstract does not mention any method used to assess risk of bias in included studies.
Describes synthesis methods: pooled as MD, SMD, or RR with 95% CIs using R software, subgroup analysis, and leave-one-out analysis.
Reports 18 RCTs with 1010 patients included in the meta-analysis.
Reports main outcomes with SMDs, MDs, RRs, and 95% CIs, indicating direction favoring US-guided injection for VAS, function, and abduction.
No discussion of limitations of evidence such as risk of bias, inconsistency, or imprecision is provided in the abstract.
Provides interpretation: US-guided CS injection was effective and further research on cost-effectiveness is needed.
No funding source is mentioned in the abstract.
No registration name or registration number is provided in the abstract.

title: Effectiveness of eHealth Self-management Interventions in Patients With Heart Failure: Systematic Review and Meta-analysis.

items.ai_reason: The provided title section shows author information but no explicit title identifying this as a systematic review. Though the abstract and keywords mention systematic review, the actual title is not shown as containing 'systematic review'.
The Introduction describes HF burden, existing knowledge on eHealth/self-management, inconsistent prior reviews, and knowledge gaps, providing rationale for this review.
Explicit objective stated: 'to perform a systematic review and meta-analysis of the effectiveness of eHealth self-management interventions in patients with HF.'
Inclusion/exclusion criteria are specified using the PICO framework, and outcomes are grouped into primary (patient) and secondary (process) outcomes for synthesis.
Databases (PubMed, EMBASE, CENTRAL, CINAHL) are specified with search dates from January 1, 2011, to July 12, 2022.
Only example combined search terms are provided; full search strategies for each database including filters and limits are not presented.
The paper describes data extraction by two reviewers but does not explicitly state how many reviewers screened each record for inclusion or whether they worked independently during selection.
States 'All outcome data used for the meta-analysis were extracted by the same two independent reviewers' with disagreements resolved by a third reviewer.

Primary outcomes (all-cause readmission, all-cause mortality, HF readmission, cardiovascular mortality) and secondary outcomes (quality of life, self-care behaviors, HF knowledge, medication adherence) are listed and defined.
Lists extracted variables including author, year, country, eHealth type, target, duration, demographics, participants, setting, and intervention/control descriptions.
Cochrane RoB 2 tool specified with 5 domains; risk of bias assessed independently by two reviewers with consultation of a third reviewer.
Mantel-Haenszel odds ratio used for dichotomous outcomes and standardized mean difference (SMD) for continuous outcomes is specified.
The criteria for inclusion in each synthesis are implicit in the outcome grouping and study characteristics tables comparing against planned outcome groups.
Describes data conversion for MLHFQ quality of life scores: 'we calculated the change using the baseline score minus the final score' since lower scores indicate better quality.
Forest plots, funnel plots, and tables (Table 1, Multimedia Appendices) are used to display individual study and synthesis results.
Describes fixed-effects and random-effects models based on heterogeneity, Cochran Q and I² statistics for heterogeneity, and software (RevMan v.5.4 and R v.4.1.0).
States 'subgroup analyses of primary outcomes regarding the enrolled population setting (after discharge or ambulatory clinic) were performed' to explore heterogeneity.
Sensitivity analysis using the leave-one-out approach is described to check robustness of pooled results.
Funnel plots and Egger test were used to measure publication bias for all primary outcomes with significance threshold P<.05.
GRADE approach is described for rating certainty of evidence, including assessment of risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, and other considerations using GRADEpro.
The search results (1884 identified, 1203 after duplicates, 59 full-text reviewed, 24 included) are described with reference to a PRISMA flow diagram (Figure 1).
The paper does not cite specific studies excluded at full-text review stage with reasons for exclusion.
Table 1 cites each included study with characteristics (country, technology type, intervention target, duration, recruitment, setting), supplemented by Multimedia Appendix 1.
Figure 2 presents risk of bias assessments for each included study across all domains, with summary percentages.
Forest plots (Figures 3-10) present effect estimates with confidence intervals for each study across all outcomes.
Each synthesis section summarizes number of studies, participants, and heterogeneity; overall risk of bias summaries are provided in the risk of bias section.
Results of all meta-analyses are presented with summary estimates, 95% CIs, P values, and I² heterogeneity statistics for each outcome.
Subgroup analysis results by enrollment setting (after discharge vs ambulatory clinic) are presented with specific ORs and CIs, summarized in Multimedia Appendix 2.
Sensitivity analysis results are presented in Multimedia Appendix 3, stating 'direction and magnitude of the combined estimates did not vary markedly with the exclusion of individual studies.'
Funnel plots and Egger test results for primary outcomes are reported in Multimedia Appendix 4, stating 'No publication bias was detected for any of the primary outcomes.'
GRADE ratings (low, moderate, very low quality) are reported for each outcome throughout the results section.
The Discussion interprets findings in context of other evidence regarding eHealth implementation challenges, user acceptance, and prior research.
Limitations of included studies discussed: varied demographics, HF types, sample sizes, assessment tools, intervention periods, single-site recruitment, self-report bias.
Review process limitation discussed: 'we only searched for literature in English, excluding other languages' which may limit retrieval of related papers.
Discussion section addresses implications for practice and future research, including cost-benefit analyses, workflow integration, and need for more clinical studies.
Registration is provided: 'The full protocol of this systematic review and meta-analysis has been registered in PROSPERO (CRD42021246973).'
The protocol is accessible via PROSPERO with the registration number CRD42021246973 provided.
No amendments to the registration or protocol are described or explained.
The paper does not describe sources of financial or non-financial support for the review or any role of funders/sponsors.
Competing interests are declared: 'Conflicts of Interest: None declared.'
The paper does not explicitly report availability of data collection forms, analytic code, or data used for analyses, though Multimedia Appendices contain some characteristics and results.
Title explicitly identifies the report as 'Systematic Review and Meta-analysis.'
Abstract states the aim was to 'systematically review the evidence for the effectiveness of eHealth self-management in patients with HF.'
Specifies inclusion of RCTs comparing eHealth interventions with usual care in adult patients with HF.
Lists EMBASE, PubMed, CENTRAL, and CINAHL databases with search dates from January 1, 2011, to July 12, 2022.
States the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool (RoB 2) was used to assess risk of bias.
Describes meta-analyses performed using RevMan v.5.4 and R v.4.1.0, with GRADE used to rate certainty of evidence.
Reports 24 RCTs with 9634 participants met inclusion criteria.
Provides pooled ORs with 95% CIs and P values for multiple outcomes including all-cause mortality, cardiovascular mortality, readmissions, and medication adherence.
Abstract notes GRADE quality levels but does not explicitly summarize limitations of the evidence such as risk of bias, inconsistency, or imprecision.
Concludes eHealth self-management interventions could benefit patients with HF and calls for more high-quality studies.
No mention of funding source in the abstract.
No registration name or registration number is provided in the abstract.

title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of elastic stockings for prevention of thrombosis after orthopedic surgery.

items.ai_reason: The title is not provided in the text, but the paper is identified as a meta-analysis in the background. No explicit title mentioning 'systematic review' is shown.
Introduction describes DVT, the role of elastic stockings, and notes that 'results of such studies vary and the preventive effect...remains unclear,' establishing rationale.
The objective is stated: 'to investigate the preventive effect of elastic stockings on DVT after orthopedic surgery by literature search and meta-analysis.'
Explicit inclusion and exclusion criteria are listed, including study type, subjects, intervention, and outcome. Subgroups were grouped by surgery type (ankle vs hip/knee).
PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane databases are specified, but the date when each source was last searched is not provided.
Only keywords are mentioned ('stockings', 'deep vein thrombosis', 'compression stockings', 'DVT'). Full search strategies with filters and Boolean operators for each database are not presented.
States 'Two researchers searched the databases,' but does not specify whether screening was done independently or describe the selection process in detail.
States 'The researchers read the full text,' listing extracted data items, but does not specify how many reviewers extracted data, independent work, or processes for confirming data.
The outcome is the incidence of DVT, diagnosed by biphasic ultrasound, as specified in inclusion criteria and data collection section.
Lists variables extracted: authors, year, number of interventions, sex ratio, age, surgical procedure, intervention methods, DVT incidence.
Risk of bias assessed using Cochrane Handbook criteria across six aspects (random sequence generation, allocation concealment, blinding, incomplete outcome, selective reporting, other biases).
Specifies odds ratio (OR) with 95% CI as the effect measure for DVT incidence.
The paper does not explicitly describe a process for deciding which studies were eligible for each synthesis beyond subgroup grouping by surgery type.
States that 'the index expressed by % in all studies was converted into actual numbers of cases.'
Describes use of forest plots to display results and tables to organize extracted data.
Describes Mantel-Haenszel method, random/fixed effect models based on heterogeneity, I² analysis and Q validation, and Revman 5.3.5 software.
Describes subgroup analysis by surgical type (ankle surgery vs. hip/knee arthroplasty) to explore heterogeneity.
Describes sensitivity analysis using a case-by-case elimination method when heterogeneity was detected.
States 'a funnel plot was used to represent publication bias' in the statistical analysis section.
No methods such as GRADE or other certainty/confidence assessment approaches are described.
Reports 90 studies initially screened, 7 finally included with 3,116 patients, and references Figure 1 flow chart of literature screening.
No specific studies are cited as excluded with reasons after full-text review.
Table 1 presents characteristics of each included study (authors, year, number, sex, age, surgery, intervention, control, DVT events) with citations.
Table 2 presents risk of bias assessment for each included study across six Cochrane domains.
Table 1 presents DVT events (T/C) for each study, and forest plots (Figures 2, 3) display effect estimates with confidence intervals.
Discussion briefly summarizes characteristics of studies contributing to each subgroup synthesis (ankle surgery vs hip/knee arthroplasty).
Presents summary estimates: OR=0.59, 95% CI (0.34, 1.03), Z=1.84, P=0.07 overall; subgroup results with Z and P values; heterogeneity I² values reported.
Reports subgroup analysis results showing heterogeneity between subgroups (I²=70.4%) and homogeneous within subgroups.
Presents results of sensitivity analysis: exclusion of Takahashi et al. resulted in homogeneity (I²=0%, P=0.46).
Funnel plot analysis of publication bias is reported, showing significant asymmetry suggesting publication bias risk (Figure 4).
No formal certainty of evidence assessment (e.g., GRADE) is presented for any outcome.
Discussion interprets results in the context of other meta-analyses (Sachdeva et al., Hajibandeh et al.) and other studies.
Discusses limitations such as heterogeneity in elastic stocking types, varying intervention duration, and publication bias risk.
Acknowledges not distinguishing specific types of elastic stockings used and not recording intervention duration in the review process.
States implications: need to combine elastic stockings with anticoagulant drugs and other physical therapies for DVT prevention.
No registration information is provided, nor is it stated that the review was not registered.
No information on protocol accessibility or statement that no protocol was prepared.
No amendments to registration or protocol are mentioned.
States 'Funding: None' in the Acknowledgments section.
States 'The authors have no conflicts of interest to declare' in the Footnote section.
No statement about availability of data collection forms, extracted data, analytic code, or other materials.
The title explicitly identifies the report as 'A systematic review and meta-analysis'.
The abstract states the objective: 'To investigate the preventive effect of elastic stockings on deep vein thrombosis (DVT) after orthopedic surgery'.
The abstract mentions only RCTs related to elastic stockings and DVT from 2008 onwards but does not specify detailed inclusion/exclusion criteria such as population, comparators, or outcomes.
PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane are specified as information sources, though exact last search dates are not given ('from 2008 to date').
No method for assessing risk of bias in included studies is mentioned in the abstract.
The abstract describes synthesis methods: Revman 5.3.5, heterogeneity assessment (I²), random/fixed effects models, subgroup analysis, sensitivity analysis, forest and funnel plots.
The abstract reports 7 studies included with a total of 3,116 patients and notes subgroups by surgery type (ankle vs hip/knee arthroplasty).
Main outcome results are reported with OR=0.59, 95% CI (0.34, 1.03), Z-values, P-values, and subgroup effect sizes indicating direction favoring stockings for ankle surgery.
The abstract does not provide a summary of limitations of the evidence such as risk of bias, inconsistency, or imprecision.
The abstract provides interpretation: elastic stockings prevent DVT after ankle surgery but not hip/knee, and recommends combining with anticoagulants and physical therapies.
No funding source for the review is mentioned in the abstract.
No registration name or registration number is provided in the abstract.
